text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to lift the liver, providing access to the right adrenal cyst. An instrument is applied to hold the liver upwards, facilitating the dissection between the liver and the right adrenal gland.
The surgeon carefully dissects around a thin-walled, bluish adrenal cyst on the right side, approximately five centimeters in size, taking care not to rupture it.

The surgeon performs dissection between the upper pole of the right kidney and the adrenal cyst. The upper pole of the kidney is visible on the left side of the surgical field, as the surgeon identifies the correct plane between these structures.
Small bleeding vessels are coagulated as the dissection progresses towards the superior part of the adrenal cyst. The chromaffin tissue is clearly visible on the surface of the cyst.
The surgeon carefully identifies the adrenal vein, preparing to ligate it as part of the adrenalectomy procedure. The adrenal artery is not visible in the surgical field.
The adrenal cyst is punctured, causing fluid to escape and the cyst to collapse visibly.
The surgeon identifies a vessel on the inferior aspect of the adrenal gland, which is likely not the adrenal vein. The vessel is carefully ligated between clips and divided.
The surgeon identifies the lumen of a vessel, revealing the site of the adrenal vein. Dissection is performed around the adrenal vein, and a ligar clip is applied to secure it.

The surgeon applies a third clip to the adrenal vein but notices insufficient space between the second and third clips, opting not to cut the vein at this time. The dissection continues, focusing on separating the upper pole of the kidney from the adrenal cyst.
The surgeon places a clip on a small vein to prevent potential oozing after cutting. The superior aspect of the adrenal cyst is prepared for removal.

The surgeon applies clips to control bleeding tissue at the inferior aspect of the liver after removing the adrenal specimen. Hemostasis is performed, addressing small tears and visible signature marks on the tissue.
